office:text.text:p: Fully Reconstructed Ideal Text

text:note-body.text:p: Var.: àga-ús.
Vars.: kala-ga-me-en lugal-mu ga-ab-ús; kala-ga-me-en àga-ús-zu hé-me-en.

office:text.text:p: 12.a gub-ba-àm a mu-da-ak-e
15.dub-sar me-en na-rú-a ab-sar-e
16.inim ugnim-ma mu-da-[…]
27.dumu nu-mu-un-kuå-me-en lú èn-tar-re la-ba-an-tuku

text:note-body.text:p: No two manuscripts agree on the first two words of the line.

office:text.text:p: 1. Speak to my king:
2. To my mountain goat, fair of limb,
3. Eagle-clawed highland horse,
4. To my date palm growing in a sacred place, laden with glistening dates,
5. Say moreover:
6/8. Saying (the words of) Abaindasa, officer of the armed forces,
7. Who, (to obtain) his master’s favor, is a constant delight to his master’s heart:

text:p.text:span: 9. Being strong, I want to follow my king,

text:note-body.text:p: Vars.: I am strong, I want to follow my king; I am strong, so let me be your soldier.

office:text.text:p: 10.Having vision, I want to go in your vanguard,
11.At your command please let me be your messenger!
12.Even when water is still, I can make water flow;

text:p.text:span: ven when the wind is still, I can winnow grain;

office:text.text:p: 14.Even when the boat is still, I can row!
15.I am a (trained) scribe—I can inscribe a stele!
16.I can … the orders of the army.
17.The orders of the assembly …
18.As if I were planting a tree in my own woodlot, I kneel in the dirt;
19.As if someone had managed to tie me to my own chair, my hands are tied with rope;
20.In my own city, where I used to dress in fine clothing, I am forced to wear dirty rags;
21.Forced to wash in clods of dirt, there is dirt on my face.

text:p.text:span: 22.A beast devours cadavers but then retreats,
23.Even after the king of beasts makes a kill, he slackens his jaw;

text:note-body.text:p: Var: it spares some remains.

text:p.text:span: 24.Even after the canebrake is consumed by fire, the pond remains (intact),

text:note-body.text:p: Var.: it spares some ponds.

office:text.text:p: 25.And even Utu, after consuming (offerings of) ghee and cheese, still reaches out to (accept offerings from) a pauper’s table;
26.(But now) my life hangs by a thread; please take my hand!
27.I am a widow’s son; I have no one to show concern for me.

text:p.text:span: 28.Ah but me—when will Åulgi, my king, restore me to my position?

office:text.text:p: 29.May my king show his concern for me and restore me to my prosperous position!

text:p.text:span: . The metaphors of the opening lines are not directly paralleled in any other composition but the general tenor is consistent with the kind of imagery that is found in Åulgi hyms, especially in the opening lines of
máå hur-saÑ-Ñá ki ùr-bi-åè húb sar-sar-re-gin
a mountain goat running to its lair (I entered the Ekishnugal).”
in the sense of “troops” in Sumerian was established by C. Wilcke,
appears as a logogram in Old Babylonian texts, standing for
, who are the “Truppen der Armee des Königs.” To my knowledge, it is not attested earlier, and therefore its usage in the correspondence concerning Abaindasa may very well be anachronistic.
53, read the first three signs as

but collations do not support this reading. This line, which is absent from all Nippur manuscripts, as well as one from Ur, contains a number of grammatical problems. No two sources have the same exact text:

office:text.text:p: Ur1saÑ.ki zalag lugal.ani.ak.åe åag lugal.ani.ra dug.dug.e.ra
Ur3saÑ.ki zalag lugal.ani.ak.ta åag lugal.ani.a dug.dug.e.ra
X1saÑ.ki zalag lugal.ani.ak.e åag lugal.ani dug.dug.e.ra
X2saÑ.ki zalag lugal.ani.ak.åe åag lugal.ani dug.dug.e.ra

text:p.text:span: The most problematical element is the final /

/; most likely this is a misplaced dative, which does not belong here, as this line pertains to the sender of the message, Abaindasa, and not to the recipient. It is also possible that it is the rare /
/ ending encountered in ArÅ1: 3 (letter 1); see the commentary to that line.

. This line has many variants that do not show any obvious clustering. The main text follows N2; N8 is broken but may have been similar. All other sources have a different first part of the line. The three Ur texts differ: Ur1 is similar to N1 but Ur2 omits the line and Ur3, in concert with X1, has
, “Because I am strong, let me be your bodyguard.”
. These lines are difficult as they are without good parallels and are rife with variants. On the one hand one is tempted to understand the repeated
as “to be appointed to work/a position,” and translate:

office:text.text:p: He who assigned to water duty must know how to do water work,

text:p.text:span: He who is assigned to the barley sheaves
He who assigned to boat duty must know how to
A somewhat different approach was taken recently by P. Attinger,

by “est à disposition.” I would not rule out either interpretation. The translation offered here—not without misgivings—is influenced, in connection with line 14, by the parallel metaphor in W. L. Lambert,
ina me ni-æu-ti lu-u gi-åal-li at-ta

, “in still waters be my oar.” Further support comes from the Susa lentil Su2, which may have an Akkadian translation on the reverse. The last two lines seem to read (the text requires detailed personal inspection):
. The line is highly problematical. Ur1 has
, which is OB Sumerian for “to winnow.” As M. Civil,
96 has shown, the Ur III term for this activity is
; in manuscripts Ur3 and Su1 the verb is
), which is most probably to be interpreted as

in N2 are clearly erroneous, and are influenced by the previous line and the beginning of this one. The confusion may be due to the lexical difference between Ur III and OB usage. Moreover, it is difficult to explain IM at the beginning of the line. The obvious interpretation is
, “wind,” as that would explain the interpretation offered by the scribe of Ur3, who wanted to see winnowing here. This does not explain the use of the verb
) here; it may very well be that that at some point IM was incorrectly interpreted as
barley),” actually a loan from Sumerian
the basis would have been lexical:
12 207]), which normally means “liar, criminal.” Note the Akkadian translation of line in Su2:
. It is possible that a faint echo of these lines is found in the somewhat garbled passage in

office:text.text:p: I am a scribal graduate, I am … to serve my king,

text:p.text:span: I am able to draw up in good form my king’s commands, and to remind my king of what he has forgotten.

text:note-body.text:p: See B. Foster, Before the Muses 223.

text:p.text:span: , “clean,” can be written with four different but visually often indistinguishable signs: most often it is or
but sometimes it can be written

s.v. In OB school texts the difference between the first two is often difficult to discern; when the end part of a sign is not well preserved, one cannot establish which one was actually used. This is the case in N1, N2, and N9.
has been translated as “to depart, retreat” (F. Karahashi,
83, with previous literature). The verb has a more nuanced meaning, “to
retreat, withdraw, turn tail,” and is used almost exclusively of animals or inanimate subjects. Contrast the marked rare example of an animate subject in the hymn
, “but I did not retreat from (the sound) of their roar!”

is presumed a mythological beast, often translated as “dragon.” In poetry this word occurs in contexts in which this meaning makes little sense but translators still insist on it. In magical texts and poetry a distinction is made between
, which refer to a snake-like beast, and
, which is a metaphor for “lion,” perhaps idiomatically equivalent to English “King of Beasts.” It is used separately or paired with
in hendiadys constructions. See, for example,
, “I am a fierce-eyed lion, born of the ‘King of Beasts’.” Instructive is the passage in

text:note-body.text:p: For this line see also Šulgi Hymn B 59 and possibly Šulgi Hymn C B1.

office:text.text:p: Even the lion, the p., ‘King of Beasts’ of the desert,
Was lying down in sweet sleep.
The matter of the words for “lion” in ancient Near Eastern languages, particularly in Sumerian and Akkadian is quite complex; I deal with this in a forthcoming article.

text:p.text:span: is usually used with a human (or divine) agent and clearly means “to kill, to beat to death;” see the examples in F. Karahashi,
138–39. The only other reference in which the action is performed by a
lion suggests that it refers to the attack of the animal:

office:text.text:p: The waters attack the King at the prow of the boat
Like a wolf pack,
The waters strike at Enki at the stern of the boat
Like lion(s).

text:p.text:span: I know of only one other occurrence of
in the lexical entry Sag A iii 36 [
1 22]) where the Akkadian is the equally obscure

, “Verschonung,” is otherwise only attested in first-millennium personal names. The meaning seems to be, “dropping from the mouth,” idiomatic for “to spare.” The broader metaphorical frame of reference links this to a passage in a Sin-iddinam inscription (Frayne 1990 E4.2.9.1: 79–83)
an eden-na, piriÑ åu ba-an-zi-ga, Ñìri-gin-na ba-an-gub, íb-tag
, “In the high desert, the lion, having risen, stood on the pathway so as to be able to kill stragglers/deserters.”

in both lines is due to the quasi-homonymic nature of the verbs but also reflects the aesthetic that dictates the repetition of the same word in two consecutive lines. The fairly consistent difference between Nippur and elsewhere is not significant, as most texts from Nippur are badly preserved at this point.
is used here in the sense of Akkadian
, “to take care, be concerned with (a person, situation),” (
P 166 173) rather than in the more commonly attested meaning, “to ask, inquire,” (Akkadian
], “May my king show his concern for me and restore me to my position.”
Line 27, which is only present in two sources, Ur1 and X2, must be compared with
, “I have no one to show concern for me.” Note that Ur1 is inscribed on a collective tablet that also included SEpM 19 (col. i 1’–4’).
often occurs in the final lines of other literary texts, most notably in eråahuÑa’s, see already M. Civil,
89. It is often translated literally but it means “to be favorably disposed to once again.” In epistolary literature see
Letter of Gudea to his God
lú kúr-ra-zu nu-me-en åà-zu ki-bi ha-ma-ab-gi
“O my god, I am not your enemy, so please be well disposed to me once again!”

office:text.text:p: Sources

text:note-body.text:p: The surface of the tablet is worn and made difficult to read by a cover of shellac for preservation.

office:text.text:p: X1 = Crozer 206a i
X2 = YBC 6458

text:p.text:span: N1 collated on cast; N4, collated by S. Tinney and on print photographs provided by Manfred Krebernik and on digital photographs provided by Jerrold Cooper. Su2 collated by

office:text.text:p: Tablet typology: compilation tablets (Type I): N5, N6, Ur1, X1, the rest are all Type III, except Su1, Su2, which are Type IV lentils.

text:p.text:span: No other text in CKU is subject to the degree of textual manipulation that was allowed for Abaindasa’s letter of petition.

Leaving aside the Susa sources, the letter is preserved on four compilation tablets: two from Nippur (N4, N6), one from Ur (Ur1, and one of unknown origin (X1). There are twelve Type III manuscripts, that is one-day

exercises and although some are in fragmentary state, it seems that they contained some version of he complete composition. Unlike any other letter, AbÅ1 seems completely fluid; students/teachers appear to choose at will what lines they will use and which they will omit. Not all the pieces are completely preserved but within what we have, only three tablets contain exactly the same lines, N5, N7, and N10 but since all three are incomplete, one cannot be certain that they were identical in content. This textual flexibility is not limited to Nippur. The three tablets from Ur are subject to the same lack of uniformity. Significantly, the three sources from that city were found in different houses, Ur1 at No. 7 Quiet St., and the other two at No. 1 Broad St., and yet the variations do not cluster according to place of origin.

text:note-body.text:p: In the table - = line broken, + = line present, ++ = line present and followed by one or more additional lines, # = line omitted.

text:section.text:p: To provide a flavor of the difference between the preserved manuscripts, I offer a translation of an idealized shorter version (the line numbers are those of the full edition):
2. To my mountain goat, fair of limb,
4. To my date palm growing in a sacred place, laden with glistening dates,
6/8. Saying (the words of) Abaindasa, officer of the armed forces,
18. As if I were planting a tree in my own woodlot, I kneel in the dirt;
19. As if someone had managed to tie me to my own chair, my hands are tied with rope;
20. In my own city, where I used to dress in fine clothing, I am forced to wear dirty rags;
21. Forced to wash in clods of dirt, there is dirt on my face.

text:p.text:span: 22. A beast devours cadavers but then retreats,

text:section.text:p: 23. Even after the king of beasts makes a kill, he slackens his jaw;
24. Even after the canebrake is consumed by fire, the pond remains;
25. And even Utu, after consuming (offerings of) ghee and cheese, still reaches out to (accept offerings from) a pauper’s table;

text:p.text:span: 28. Ah but me—when will Åulgi, my king, restore me to my position?

text:section.text:p: X3 + + + o o o o o
N7 o o o o o . + . . o o

text:note-body.text:p: See p. 000 below.
A modern piece of clay with etched in wedges has been inserted here.
Probably truncated úr.

text:section.text:p: N7 o . + + o o o . + + . o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: Preceded by an erased a.

text:section.text:p: N8 o o o . o o

text:p.text:span: X2 . o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: Followed by traces of two lines.

text:section.text:p: N8 o o o . + o o o

text:p.text:span: X1 has an additional line: A DIŠ

text:note-body.text:p: Followed by erased NI.
The line ends with four squeezed signs that I cannot read; it seems to be a verb beginning with ga-.

text:p.text:span: N4 . . . . o o o o o

text:section.text:p: Ur3 o o o x x im-ma-ak-en(after 13)

text:note-body.text:p: See full transliteration below. The final sign seems to be da but could be a combination of da and ak.

text:p.text:span: Ur3 o o o o še mu-un-sì-ge

text:note-body.text:p: Preceded by an erased sign.

text:section.text:p: N2 o . + + x o o o o o o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: The scribe has trouble with úh and began to write something else, erased it, and then wrote the right sign. He then repeated the line, once again with a problematical úh, partially erasing the offending sign: inim pu-x-úh-ru-um-m[a …].

text:p.text:span: + + . o o o o o o o o o o
N4 has another line after 17: inim sa

text:section.text:p: N2 o o . . . . . o o o o o o o o o
N4 + + + + + - + . o o o o o o o o

text:p.text:span: N7 o . + + . . o o o o .

N9 . + + + + - + un o o o o o o o o

N10 o o o + + + + . o o/o o . + + +

text:section.text:p: Followed by another line in X1.

text:p.text:span: . . o o o o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: Erasure, or broken ára.
Followed by three additional lines; see p. 000 below.

text:p.text:span: X1 oo o o o o o o o o o o o o o o o o .

text:note-body.text:p: Written as ka.

text:section.text:p: N8 o o o o . + -. o o o o o . o o o

text:p.text:span: In N6, Ur1, X1, and probably in N5 followed by SEpM2.

text:section.text:p: Su1 is a round tablet from Susa with an elementary exercise based on this letter:
On the right of the rectangle, written horizontally:
The central part of the reverse is very worn and only traces of signs remain. Lines 7–9 should correspond to the second part of line 3 and the first part of line 4.

text:p.text:span: Su2 is a round practice tablet with lines 13 and 12 a repeated set of lines on the obverse and at least six lines on the reverse. A fragment on the left edge is now missing; this transliteration is based both on the copy in
27 207 and the collation of the tablet in its present state by Mrs. Mernoush Malyeri. In the matrix the text is entered only once.

The reverse is difficult to read. The first four preserved lines are illegible to me and would require detailed study of the original tablet, which is unavailable to me at present. Many of the Susa practice tablet of type have on the reverse a syllabic version of the Sumerian lines from the obverse, followed by an Akkadian translation, and it may be that this lentil
was of that kind. The first lines of the obverse were therefore probably syllabic; then came the translations of the two lines. I can only read the final preserved lines:
N8 has three unique lines between 25 and 28:
